RAG: Retrieval-Augmented Generation — a technique where an AI finds relevant documents first, then uses them to generate an answer. Like an open-book exam instead of memorising everything.
LLM: Large Language Model — an AI system trained on massive amounts of text that can understand and generate human language. Examples: GPT-4, Claude, Llama.
hallucination: When an AI confidently generates information that sounds right but is actually made up. RAG reduces this by grounding answers in real documents.
fine-tuning: Retraining an AI model on your specific data so it 'memorises' new information. More expensive and slower to update than RAG.
context window: The maximum amount of text an AI can process at once, measured in tokens. Think of it as the AI's working memory — bigger window means more text at once.
token: A piece of text that the AI processes — roughly 3/4 of a word in English. 'Chatbot' is 1 token. 'Retrieval-Augmented Generation' is about 4 tokens.
chunk: A piece of a document, typically paragraph-sized, that contains enough context to be useful on its own. Documents get split into chunks before being stored.
chunking: The process of splitting a document into smaller, meaningful pieces (chunks) that an AI can process individually.
overlap: When splitting text into chunks, the number of characters or tokens shared between consecutive chunks. Prevents ideas from being cut in half.
embedding: A list of numbers (a vector) that captures the meaning of a piece of text. Similar meanings produce similar numbers, letting computers measure how related two texts are.
vector: An ordered list of numbers, like [0.12, -0.05, 0.89]. In RAG, vectors represent the meaning of text — each number captures one aspect of the meaning.
embedding model: A specialised AI that converts text into vectors (embeddings). Different from a chat model — it doesn't generate text, it just measures meaning.
cosine similarity: A way to measure how similar two vectors are by looking at the angle between them. Score of 1.0 means identical meaning, 0 means unrelated.
vector database: A database optimised for storing vectors and quickly finding the most similar ones. Regular databases can't do similarity search efficiently.
semantic search: Finding documents by meaning rather than exact keyword matches. 'How to fix a bug' would match 'debugging techniques' even though they share no words.
BM25: A keyword-based search algorithm that ranks documents by how well they match search terms. Fast and effective, but doesn't understand meaning.
hybrid search: Combining semantic search (by meaning) with keyword search (by exact terms). Usually gives better results than either alone.
top-K: The number of results returned from a search. 'Top-5' means returning the 5 most similar chunks.
re-ranking: A second pass over search results using a more accurate (but slower) model to pick the truly best matches from a larger candidate set.
HNSW: Hierarchical Navigable Small World — an algorithm that vector databases use to find similar vectors extremely fast, even among millions.
RAGAS: An open-source framework for evaluating RAG systems. Measures faithfulness, answer relevancy, context precision, and context recall.
faithfulness: Whether the AI's answer is actually supported by the retrieved documents, or whether it's making things up.
MMR: Maximum Marginal Relevance — a technique that ensures search results are diverse, not just relevant. Avoids returning 5 chunks that all say the same thing.
